title: Diogenes Laertius Lives of Philosophers Synopsis

enrichment.summary: Kyle reviews Diogenes Laertius's 'Lives of Eminent Philosophers' (c. 200 AD), walking through the history and timeline of ancient Greek philosophy from the Seven Sages through Socrates, the Cynics, Stoics, Peripatetics, and Epicurus. He shares his views on how scientifically advanced the ancient Greeks were, draws parallels between Greek philosophical sayings and the teachings of Jesus Christ, and argues that ancient Greek knowledge (possibly derived from Egypt) prefigured discoveries credited to thinkers like Isaac Newton.
enrichment.speaker: kyle
topics: book review: Lives of Eminent Philosophers

stances.position: far more advanced than mainstream credit gives them — they correctly described cosmology, atomic structure, gravity, and weather
likely rediscovered ancient Greek concepts rather than inventing them from scratch
largely a continuation of ancient Greek philosophical sayings, not just Hebrew tradition
smart people always knew the earth was round; only common people believed it was flat
had high technology and scientific knowledge that Greek philosophers (especially Pythagoras) drew from
essentially the same social dynamics — class, materialism, greed, law, and meritocracy all existed then as now
a barbarian worldview that Socrates helped dismantle by showing it breaks down in a lawful society
